sentence: I’ve always loved maple syrup making time.
Maple syrup making time has always been my favourite.
I have always had a fondness for the maple syrup making season.
Thank goodness John is with us or we would have lost our way for certain.
It is a good thing John is with us, otherwise we would surely have lost our way.
Without John here we would certainly have gone astray — thank goodness he came.
One old lady passed me by just now and muttered “Tory cowards” under her breath, but not so much under her breath that I could not hear.
An old lady walked past me just now and grumbled "Tory cowards" under her breath — though not quietly enough that I missed it.
Just now an old lady went by muttering "Tory cowards," not quite under her breath enough to keep me from hearing.

I could not stop my tongue from wagging, asking him how Hannah was, did her family have their cabin built yet, and a thousand other questions, not to mention, how did he ever get a horse and from where?
I couldn't stop myself from peppering him with questions — how was Hannah, had her family finished building their cabin yet, and above all, where on earth did he get a horse?
My tongue ran away with me as I asked after Hannah, whether her family's cabin was done, and a hundred other things, not least of all how he had come by a horse.
Just imagine, next spring we will have lilacs in the doorway again.
To think — come next spring we will have lilacs blooming in the doorway once more.
Next spring we shall have lilacs in the doorway again — just imagine it.
Angus and Duncan will help us get our place built, then they will build shanties of their own on their properties.
Angus and Duncan will lend us a hand getting our place built before turning to put up their own shanties on their land.
Once they have helped us build our place, Angus and Duncan will go on to build their own shanties on their properties.
Oh, how I hope we can travel up to Canada together.
How I hope we will be able to travel up to Canada together.
My greatest hope is that we can all make the journey up to Canada as one.
Best of all, Hannah and her family brought their tent and are staying over.
The best part of all is that Hannah and her family brought their tent and are spending the night.
Hannah and her family brought their tent and are staying over — and that is the best news of everything.
Mrs. Livingstone, whose family lives just a few cabins away, came over this morning with a crock of yeast starter so that we can make our own bread.
This morning Mrs. Livingstone, whose family lives just a few cabins down, stopped by with a crock of yeast starter so we can bake our own bread.
A few cabins away lives Mrs. Livingstone's family, and she came over this morning carrying a crock of yeast starter for us to use for our bread.
Now, instead of grumbling, people are beginning to make plans and even look forward to our journey upriver.
People have stopped grumbling and started making plans, even looking forward to the journey upriver.
Rather than grumbling as before, people are now making plans and even finding themselves eager for the upriver journey.
The men were all laughing and shouting to each other as they began to gather up all the brushwood and small trees that Father and Angus and Duncan had cut down.
The men laughed and called out to one another as they set about gathering up all the brushwood and small trees that Father, Angus, and Duncan had cut.
Laughing and shouting to each other, the men began collecting the brushwood and small trees that Father and Angus and Duncan had felled.
I hope Father will put in windows.
I am hoping Father will put windows in.
Windows — I do hope Father includes those.
They are going to settle in a big town in Québec called Montréal.
They plan to settle in a large town in Québec by the name of Montréal.
Their destination is Montréal, a big town in Québec.
I knew that many of the soldiers’ families have been coming up during this past summer and I supposed his might as well.
I had known that many soldiers' families had been arriving throughout the summer, so I assumed his had come up as well.
Knowing that soldiers' families had been making their way up all summer, I figured his family must have done the same.
They did give him ammunition, though, so he will be able to hunt.
They did give him ammunition at any rate, so hunting will still be possible for him.
At least they provided him with ammunition, which means he will be able to go out hunting.
More good news is that the cabin we have been allotted is very close to the Ross family’s.
More good news: the cabin we have been given is very near the Ross family's.
To add to the good news, the cabin assigned to us sits very close to where the Ross family is.
There are quite a few Scottish families here, so Hannah and I and the other Scottish children will be going singing at the doors of every house, just as we used to do in Albany.
Since there are quite a few Scottish families here, Hannah and I and the other Scottish children will go from door to door singing, just as we did back in Albany.
There being a good number of Scottish families about, Hannah and I and the other Scottish children plan to go singing at every house, the way we always did in Albany.
My life has settled down into such a routine — sometimes I even forget that I haven’t lived here forever.
My days have fallen into such a steady routine that I sometimes forget I have not always lived here.
Life here has become so routine that I can almost believe I have never lived anywhere else.
There are five sailors manning the boat: four to row and one to steer, but the sailors are happy because the wind is so strong that they can run with the sail up and do not have to row.
Five sailors man the boat — four at the oars, one at the helm — but they are in good spirits because the wind is strong enough to fill the sail and spare them from rowing.
The boat has five sailors: four rowing and one steering, though with the wind as strong as it is they can run under sail and leave the oars alone, which pleases them greatly.
Father is making furniture for us.
Father is busy making furniture for the family.
We are getting furniture, made by Father himself.

“Merci,”
 we said, and the woman fairly beamed back at us.
"Merci," we said, and the woman's face lit up with a broad smile.
We offered our "Merci" and the woman beamed back at us with obvious delight.
Angus is so enthusiastic about starting out all over again here in Canada.
Angus cannot hide his eagerness to start fresh here in Canada.
There is no mistaking how excited Angus is about beginning anew here in Canada.
He is building bunks against the walls for Jamie and me up in the loft, and two larger beds in the main room for Mother and himself and for Grannie.
Up in the loft he is building bunks along the walls for Jamie and me, and down in the main room two larger beds for Mother, himself, and Grannie.
He is putting together bunks for Jamie and me in the loft and two bigger beds in the main room for Mother, Grannie, and himself.
I was seated next to Mother and for the first time since we were forced out of our home, I saw her face relax and soften.
Sitting beside Mother, I watched her face — for the first time since we were driven from our home — finally loosen and grow calm.
I was next to Mother and I noticed that, for the first time since we lost our home, the tension left her face.
I do hope we will be able to stay together.
I truly hope we will manage to stay together.
My hope is that we will not be separated.
This is actually a very pleasant way to travel.
Travelling this way is actually quite pleasant.
As it turns out, this is a rather agreeable way to make the journey.
Grannie is the only one of us born in Scotland, and she came here as a babe in her mother’s arms, but she still has a burr to her voice.
Of all of us, only Grannie was born in Scotland — she came over as an infant in her mother's arms — yet she still carries a burr in her voice.
Grannie is the sole one among us who is Scottish-born, arriving here as a baby held in her mother's arms, and yet her voice still holds that Scottish burr.
The Stantons and the Hooples came too, and I was glad to see Annie and Flossie, though not as glad as I was to see Hannah.
The Stantons and the Hooples came along too, and it was good to see Annie and Flossie, even if seeing Hannah made me happier still.
Annie and Flossie were there with the Stantons and Hooples, and I was glad of it, though nothing compared to my joy at seeing Hannah.
After much conniving, Uncle Andrew and Uncle Allan managed to get land near ours.
After a great deal of scheming, Uncle Andrew and Uncle Allan managed to secure land close to ours.
Uncle Andrew and Uncle Allan did quite a bit of conniving, but in the end they got land right near ours.
I asked him if he didn’t miss Albany at all and he just said, “Not a bit.” I don’t really believe him, but I guess he sure wouldn’t want to go back after having fought on the British side.
I asked whether he missed Albany even a little, and he said simply, "Not a bit." I don't quite believe him, but I suppose he'd never want to go back after fighting on the British side.
"Not a bit," he told me when I asked if he missed Albany — though I'm not entirely convinced, since going back would be impossible for someone who fought for the British.
“Canada is our country now,” he said finally, “We belong here.
"Canada is our country now," he said at last. "This is where we belong."
"We belong here," he said finally. "Canada is our home now."
“We must have good shelter and at least some food of our own,” he says.
"We need proper shelter and at least a little food of our own," he says.
"Good shelter and some food of our own — that is what we must have," he says.

“We hewed our home out of the woods in Albany when we were first wed, and I see no reason why we cannot do so again.”
The words were so brave.
"We carved our home from the forest in Albany when we were newlyweds, and I see no reason we cannot do it again." Such brave words they were.
"We built our home out of the Albany woods when we first married — there is no reason we cannot do the same here." The bravery in those words struck me.
Fortunately, we have the quilts that we managed to bring with us.
Luckily, we were able to bring our quilts along with us.
It is a good thing we managed to carry the quilts when we left.
They have been here for almost three years and are so well settled in they even have a small flock of chickens and a few geese.
They have been settled here for nearly three years and are so established that they even keep a small flock of chickens and a few geese.
Nearly three years here has left them well settled — they even have chickens and geese of their own.
Mother was working in her garden this morning and Father was busy clearing as usual.
This morning Mother tended her garden while Father kept himself busy clearing, as he always does.
Father was at his usual work clearing land this morning while Mother worked in her garden.
Hannah has an Uncle Allan who is also with Sir John Johnson’s Royal Yorkers, which is why her family is going to Sorel too.
Hannah has an Uncle Allan serving with Sir John Johnson's Royal Yorkers, which is why her family is also heading to Sorel.
Because her Uncle Allan is with Sir John Johnson's Royal Yorkers, Hannah's family is bound for Sorel as well.
The boatmen sing constantly as we go along.
The boatmen keep up a constant song as we travel.
All along the way, the boatmen sing without stopping.
She carries her own load and tries to lighten mine when I am carrying the baby.
She shoulders her own load and does what she can to ease mine when I have the baby to carry.
Her own burden is hers to carry, and she still tries to take some of mine off me when I am holding the baby.
“It’s a new country with new ways, Mother,” he said, and would not let up on her, but even so I was amazed that he managed to convince her.
"It's a new country with new ways, Mother," he insisted, and kept at her until, to my amazement, she came around.
He would not let it go — "A new country, new ways, Mother" — and I was astonished that he actually managed to bring her around.
One old lady passed me by just now and muttered “Tory cowards” under her breath, but not so much under her breath that I could not hear.
A passing old lady grumbled "Tory cowards" in what she meant to be a whisper, but I heard every word.
"Tory cowards" — an old lady muttered it under her breath as she passed, just loudly enough that I caught it.
I was seated next to Mother and for the first time since we were forced out of our home, I saw her face relax and soften.
Seated next to Mother, I saw what I had not seen since we were forced from our home — her face ease and soften.
For the first time since we lost our home, I sat beside Mother and watched the worry leave her face.
I do hope we will be able to stay together.
My hope is that we will be able to remain together.
I dearly hope nothing will separate us.
I asked him if he didn’t miss Albany at all and he just said, “Not a bit.” I don’t really believe him, but I guess he sure wouldn’t want to go back after having fought on the British side.
When I asked if he missed Albany at all he said "Not a bit," though I have my doubts — especially since going back after fighting for the British would be out of the question.
He claimed not to miss Albany in the slightest, but I find that hard to believe, even if returning after siding with the British would be impossible.
“We must have good shelter and at least some food of our own,” he says.
"At the very least we need solid shelter and some food to call our own," he says.
"Proper shelter and a bit of food of our own — we cannot do without those," he says.
She carries her own load and tries to lighten mine when I am carrying the baby.
She carries her share and still tries to take some of my load when the baby is in my arms.
Even with her own load to carry, she makes an effort to help with mine whenever I have the baby.
We have a barrel of salt pork sitting outside the door (what joy!)
Outside the door sits a barrel of salt pork — what a welcome sight that is!
There is a barrel of salt pork just outside the door, and what joy that brings!
I was right to worry about the waves.
My worry about the waves turned out to be well founded.
I was right to have been anxious about those waves.
When we reach the beginning of the rapids, we passengers all disembark and take whatever is most precious to us, in case one of the boats does tip.
At the start of the rapids we all get out of the boats and take whatever matters most to us, in case any of the boats should overturn.
When we reach the rapids, the passengers disembark and carry their most precious things, just in case a boat tips over.
The summer is wearing on and they have not yet finished surveying the lots.
Summer is slipping by and the lot surveying still isn't finished.
The lots still haven't been surveyed and the summer keeps wearing on.
We walk all day with only a brief stop for a cold meal.
All day we walk, stopping only briefly for a cold meal.
We march the whole day through, with nothing but a short break for a cold meal.
Nor do we have a bootmaker here to make us new boots when the ones we are wearing give out.
There is no bootmaker here either, so when our boots wear out we will have no way to replace them.
We have no bootmaker in the settlement, which means new boots will be impossible to come by when ours give out.
The lake is huge and the waves are enormous and the boat we are sailing in is so very small!
The lake stretches out enormously, the waves are huge, and the boat carrying us seems so terribly small!
Our little boat feels very small indeed against the vast lake and its enormous waves.

When we put in to shore for our noon meal we are immediately attacked by the black flies and mosquitoes, and the flies descend in such droves to cover our food that it is hard to take a bite without crunching down on one of them.
The moment we come ashore for our midday meal, the black flies and mosquitoes are on us, and the flies swarm over the food so thickly that every bite risks crunching down on one.
We are barely ashore for our noon meal before the black flies and mosquitoes descend on us, and the food is so covered in flies that it is nearly impossible to eat without swallowing one.
Mother and Father were sleeping under a tarpaulin with baby Margaret, and Grannie was tucked away in a kind of tent that Father sets up for her.
Mother and Father slept under a tarpaulin with baby Margaret, while Grannie was tucked into the small tent Father rigs up for her.
Father had set up a kind of tent for Grannie, while he and Mother sheltered under a tarpaulin with baby Margaret.
We had been promised more clothing, too, but it seems that the difficulties of distributing clothing are too great.
We were promised additional clothing as well, but it seems distributing it is proving too difficult.
More clothing had been promised to us, though the challenges of getting it distributed appear to be too great.
Poor little Jamie will be loaded down with as much as he can bear.
Little Jamie will be loaded up with as much weight as he can manage.
Poor Jamie will have to carry as heavy a load as his small frame can bear.
He admits now that he has been very worried as to whether we were on the right track or not, and John knows this country.
He admits now to having been deeply worried about whether we were on the right path — and this is a man who knows this country.
Even John, who knows this land well, confesses he was very troubled about whether we were heading the right way.
It must be so hard for him to see all of us so happy when he had to leave his family behind in Albany — but something tells me there is more to it than that.
It must be so difficult for him to see all of us so cheerful when he had to leave his family behind in Albany — though I sense there is more to it than that.
Watching us all so happy while his own family remains in Albany must be painful for him — but something tells me the full story goes deeper.
There were times the last two days when I did not think I could walk another step.
There were moments over the past two days when I truly believed I could not take another step.
More than once these last two days I felt sure my legs would not carry me any further.
To lose your country is bad enough, but to lose your family as well!
Losing your homeland is terrible enough — but losing your family along with it!
It is one thing to lose your country, but to lose your family at the same time — that is too much to bear.
Now I’m remembering old Blue and missing him.
Old Blue has been on my mind and I find I miss him.
Thoughts of old Blue keep coming back to me and I miss him dearly.
Finally we were able to set up the tents.
At last we were able to get the tents up.
We managed to put the tents up in the end.
Of course, once we leave Machiche, there is no telling when we will find another school.
Once we leave Machiche, there is no knowing when we will come across another school.
There is no way of telling when we might find a school again after we move on from Machiche.
We cannot take old Blue, nor Bess — not even the chickens!
Old Blue cannot come with us, nor Bess — not even the chickens are allowed.
We must leave behind old Blue and Bess and even the chickens — none of them can come.
We’ve been on the move for two days now, heading north, and I am bone weary and sick to death.
Two days of travelling north and I am utterly exhausted and at my absolute limit.
We have been heading north for two days now and I am worn to the bone and quite fed up.
They still hurt, though, and I’m bruised all over from being jounced around so in the wagon when I couldn’t walk any more.
They are still sore, and being bounced around in the wagon when I could no longer walk has left me bruised all over.
My feet still ache, and the wagon ride when I couldn't walk anymore has left bruises all over my body.
“Why can we not just keep our wagon and make our way north by the road?” Mother pleaded when he broke the news to her.
"Why can't we just keep the wagon and travel north by road?" Mother begged when Father broke the news.
When Father told her, Mother pleaded, "Couldn't we simply take the wagon and go north by road?"
I’m sure the weather was never this dreadful back home.
I am certain the weather back home was never as dreadful as this.
The weather at home was never this bad — I am sure of it.
Johnstown is no more than a collection of tents strewn along the riverside.
Johnstown is nothing more than a scattering of tents along the riverbank.
What passes for Johnstown is just a few tents spread out along the riverside.
Oh, how I used to love this time of year!
Oh, how I used to delight in this time of year!
This season used to fill me with such happiness.
We are packing what little we have and this small cabin is in a turmoil.
We are packing up the little we own and the small cabin has been thrown into complete disorder.
Packing up our few belongings has put the small cabin into a state of turmoil.
This year we will have to make do with the rations we have received from the soldiers and what Father can shoot.
This year we will have to manage on the soldiers' rations and whatever Father can bring in from hunting.
We shall have to get by this year on the rations from the soldiers and what game Father manages to shoot.
The path we’re on dwindles down to almost nothing in places and it is very hard to make it out.
The path narrows to almost nothing in places, making it very difficult to follow.
In spots the path nearly disappears altogether and it becomes very hard to know where to go.
We will have to make do with what we wear on our backs.
We will have to get by with only the clothes on our backs.
Nothing but what we are wearing — that is all we will have to make do with.
The fare for the boat is so exorbitant, we will need the extra money to meet it,” Father answered.
"The boat fare is so outrageously expensive that we will need every extra coin to cover it," Father said.
Father explained that the boat passage costs so much that we cannot afford to spare any money — we will need it all.
